stem: Find The output of C++ Code (Program 1)
explanation: Compiled with g++ -std=c++17 and run: the program prints "Hii World". NULL is the integer constant 0, so if (NULL) is false and the else branch executes. (The source itself prints the same output.)

stem: Find The output of C++ Code (Program 2)
explanation: Compiled with g++ -std=c++17 and run: the program prints "PQR". First test: > binds tighter than &&, so it is A && (B > C) = 5 && (10 > 20) = false. Second test is an ASSIGNMENT, not a comparison: A = (B || C == 20) = (10 || 1) = 1, and 1 is truthy, so the PQR branch runs. (The source itself prints the same output.)

stem: Find The output of C++ Code
explanation: Compiled with g++ -std=c++17 and run: the output is 4, 6, 8, 10 - each on its own line. i=j++ copies j into i and then increments j, so inside the body j is always ODD (5, 7, 9, 11) and the if (j==8) continue never fires; the extra j++ in the header makes j advance by 2 per iteration, so j starts each iteration at 4, 6, 8, 10 and i takes those same values before printing.

stem: Find The output of C++ Code
explanation: Compiled with g++ -std=c++17 and run: the program prints "222". A > B (15 > 10) and A >= 15 are both true, so the nested ternary is evaluated: C > 10 is true, so it becomes A > 5 ? NULL : 1; A > 5 is true, so the value is NULL, i.e. 0 -> false -> the else branch prints 222. (The source itself prints the same output.)

stem: Find The output of C++ Code (C++ for loop)
explanation: Compiled with g++ -std=c++17 and run: the program prints "1 2 3 4 5 " (each number followed by a space). (The source itself prints the same output.)

stem: Find The output of C++ Code
explanation: Compiled with g++ -std=c++17 and run: the output is 0, 2, 4, 6 - each on its own line. i=j++ assigns the old j to i and increments j, so inside the body j is always ODD (1, 3, 5, 7) and if (j==4) continue never fires. With the header's j++ as well, j advances by 2 per iteration: it enters the body as 0, 2, 4, 6, which are exactly the values printed.

stem: What will be the output of the following C++ code?
explanation: Compiled with g++ -std=c++17 and run: the program prints 10. The semicolon right after the for-header makes the loop body EMPTY, so the loop just runs i up to 10; the following braces are a plain block that executes once, printing the final value of i. (The source prints "Answer: B", which agrees.)

stem: How many types of loops are there in C++?
explanation: There are four types of loop. They are the while, do while, nested, for the loop.

stem: What is the effect of writing a break statement inside a loop?
explanation: The source prints "Answer: A" (and states no explanation is given). break exits the enclosing loop immediately, abandoning the remaining iterations; continue is the one that skips a single iteration.

stem: How many times "Hello" is printed?
explanation: Compiled and run (wrapped in a main() with <cstdio>): "Hello" is printed 0 times. Every iteration takes either the continue (x < 5) or the break (x >= 5, first at x = 5), so the printf after the if/else is unreachable - it is dead code.

stem: How many times the while loop will be executed?
explanation: Compiled and run with an iteration counter: the body executes 125 times. j takes the odd values 1, 3, 5, ... , 249 (the last one <= 250), i.e. (249 - 1) / 2 + 1 = 125 iterations; the loop exits with j = 251.

stem: Which of the following is not logical operator?
explanation: & is the bitwise AND operator, not a logical operator. The logical operators in C/C++ are && (AND), || (OR) and ! (NOT).

stem: In mathematics and computer programming, which is the correct order of mathematical operators? (Hint: BODMAS)
explanation: BODMAS = Brackets, Orders, Division, Multiplication, Addition, Subtraction. Of the four operators listed, the evaluation order is therefore Division, Multiplication, Addition, Subtraction.

stem: What will be the output of the program?
explanation: Compiled and run: the output is 6. The trailing semicolon on the for-header makes the loop body empty, so the loop only increments i until the condition i<=5 fails (i = 6). The printf is NOT part of the loop; it runs once afterwards and prints the final value 6.

stem: What will be the output of the program?
explanation: Compiled and run: the output is C-program. a > 10 is false (5 > 10), so the ternary selects the format string "%s\n", and printf formats str with it.

stem: What will be the output of the program?

options.label: B
options.text: x and y are not equal

explanation: Compiled and run: the output is "x and y are equal". In x == y the int is promoted to float; 3.0f is exactly representable, so the comparison is true. (The usual float-comparison trap does not bite here because 3.0 has an exact binary representation.)

stem: What will be the output of the program?
explanation: Compiled with g++ -std=c++17 and run: the output is exactly "11, 13, 17, 19, " (with a trailing comma and space). The inner for has only the if(!(i%j)) break; as its body - the indented if(j > (i/j)) line belongs to the OUTER loop. The construct is a primality test, so it prints the primes in [10, 20).

stem: What will be the output of the program?
explanation: Compiled with g++ -std=c++17 and run (declaring int row, star; inside a main()): three lines of ELEVEN stars each. The '*' is printed before the break test, so star = 0..10 all print (11 stars) and the break fires at star == 10 after printing; the outer loop repeats that 3 times, each followed by endl.

stem: What will be the output of the program?
explanation:
Compiled with g++ -std=c++17 and run: a 4x4 grid. Each cell is the two digits i then j, followed by a space and a TAB (the literal is " \t" - space first), and each of the 4 rows ends with a newline:
00 	01 	02 	03 	
10 	11 	12 	13 	
20 	21 	22 	23 	
30 	31 	32 	33

stem: What will be the output of the program?
explanation:
Compiled with g++ -std=c++17 and run: five lines forming a number triangle - row i prints 1..i:
1
12
123
1234
12345

stem: The address 11.0.0.1 belongs to class …………..………….. and has a default subnet mask of …………..…………..………...
explanation: First octet = 11 -> binary 0000 1011, so the leading bit is 0. Per the source's Table 1, a leading 0 (range 1.0.0.0 - 126.255.255.255) is Class A, whose default subnet mask is 255.0.0.0 (/8): 8 network bits, 24 host bits.

stem: Your company has just been assigned the network 4.0.0.0. How many subnets and hosts-per-subnet you can create with a subnet mask of 255.255.255.0?
explanation: 4.0.0.0 -> first octet 4 -> Class A -> default mask 255.0.0.0 (/8), i.e. 8 network bits. The given mask 255.255.255.0 is /24, so 24 - 8 = 16 bits are borrowed from the host part -> 2^16 = 65,536 subnets (the source counts subnets as 2^borrowed, as in its own 2^8 example). Host bits left = 32 - 24 = 8 -> 2^8 - 2 = 254 usable hosts per subnet (minus network and broadcast).

stem: Your company has just been assigned the network 130.0.0.0. How many subnets and hosts-per-subnet you can create with a subnet mask of 255.255.128.0?
explanation: 130.0.0.0 -> first octet 130 (128-191) -> Class B -> default mask 255.255.0.0 (/16), i.e. 16 network bits. The given mask 255.255.128.0 is 1111 1111.1111 1111.1000 0000.0000 0000 = /17, so 17 - 16 = 1 bit is borrowed -> 2^1 = 2 subnets. Host bits left = 32 - 17 = 15 -> 2^15 - 2 = 32,766 usable hosts per subnet.

stem: Your company has just been assigned the network 198.23.16.0. How many subnets and hosts-per-subnet you can create with a subnet mask of 255.255.255.252?
explanation: 198.23.16.0 -> first octet 198 (192-223) -> Class C -> default mask 255.255.255.0 (/24), i.e. 24 network bits. The given mask 255.255.255.252 ends in 1111 1100 = /30, so 30 - 24 = 6 bits are borrowed -> 2^6 = 64 subnets. Host bits left = 32 - 30 = 2 -> 2^2 - 2 = 2 usable hosts per subnet (the classic point-to-point /30).
